coredata.dc:title: Exploration of graphene quantum dots: Design, properties, energy storage and conversion

coredata.dc:description: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional graphene nanofragments typically with an average size ranging from 2 to 50 nm. The scientific community has taken a keen interest in these nanoparticles due to their outstanding features, which include incredible surface area, excellent conductivity, tunable fluorescence, high photostability, and superior solubility in diverse solvents. This review explores the top-down method which breaks down larger graphene structures and the bottom-up method which assembles GQDs from molecular precursors approaches for the effective synthesis of simple and doped GQDs. This paper also stresses the effect of these synthetic approaches on specific characteristics of GQDs such as quantum efficiency, edge effect, nano size range, and irradiation wavelength. In addition, this review explores the structural, morphological, electrical, electrochemical, optical, and optoelectrical properties of GQDs that make them ideal for energy applications. Furthermore, a good correlation has been built between the synthesis approaches, characteristics, and properties of GQDs for energy storage applications viz. in batteries, solar cells, fuel cells, supercapacitors, and micro-supercapacitors. However, challenges to achieve scalable synthesis, stability, uniformity, and integration into commercial devices limit their broader adoption. Addressing these issues could unlock novel applications of GQDs in electrocatalysis, hydrogen storage, and biocompatible energy systems. This review emphasizes the significant potential of GQDs in next-generation sustainable energy technologies while outlining key challenges and future research directions.

head.ce:title: Exploration of graphene quantum dots: Design, properties, energy storage and conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lovely Professional University, Punjab, 144411, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Lovely Professional University, Punjab, 144411, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional graphene nanofragments typically with an average size ranging from 2 to 50 nm. The scientific community has taken a keen interest in these nanoparticles due to their outstanding features, which include incredible surface area, excellent conductivity, tunable fluorescence, high photostability, and superior solubility in diverse solvents. This review explores the top-down method which breaks down larger graphene structures and the bottom-up method which assembles GQDs from molecular precursors approaches for the effective synthesis of simple and doped GQDs. This paper also stresses the effect of these synthetic approaches on specific characteristics of GQDs such as quantum efficiency, edge effect, nano size range, and irradiation wavelength. In addition, this review explores the structural, morphological, electrical, electrochemical, optical, and optoelectrical properties of GQDs that make them ideal for energy applications. Furthermore, a good correlation has been built between the synthesis approaches, characteristics, and properties of GQDs for energy storage applications viz. in batteries, solar cells, fuel cells, supercapacitors, and micro-supercapacitors. However, challenges to achieve scalable synthesis, stability, uniformity, and integration into commercial devices limit their broader adoption. Addressing these issues could unlock novel applications of GQDs in electrocatalysis, hydrogen storage, and biocompatible energy systems. This review emphasizes the significant potential of GQDs in next-generation sustainable energy technologies while outlining key challenges and future research directions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the 21st century, as environmental degradation and the energy crisis have gotten worse, it has become more critical than ever to develop sustainable and eco-friendly energy sources. This necessitates the creation of specific energy conversion and storage technologies that are both highly efficient and affordable. In this context, interest in the production of efficient energy sources, notably electrochemical energy storage devices, has increased recently. However, electrochemical energy storage devices still face difficulties with high power density, energy density, and long-term cycling life [1]. Due to this, research on electrochemical energy conversion and storage technologies, including fuel cells, supercapacitors, and Li-ion batteries (LIBs), has advanced quickly [2]. The type of materials used as electrodes has a significant impact on how well these energy storage devices operate. Numerous attempts, as represented by Ansari 2022, have been made to date, to suggest various types of materials and determine their practicality in building functional devices for energy storage and conversion [1]. For electrochemical cells, electrode materials fall into one of three major categories: (a) carbonaceous materials (b) TMOs (transition metal oxides), and/or (c) conductive polymers (CPs). Mudila et al., 2022, had reported the reliance of electrode material's electrochemical properties on their dimensions (0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D). Different conducting materials are utilized as propitious contenders for batteries and other electrochemical energy storage systems (EESS), which are employed as electrode materials and possess a variety of properties that make them appropriate for such an application [3]. 3D nanomaterials, such as dispersed nanoparticles, multilayered nanotubes, bulk powder, etc., have all three dimensions outside of the nanoscale. Examples of 2D nanomaterials include graphene, nanofilms, nanolayers, nanocoating, etc., which are often sheet-like structures that are outside the nano range. Out of all, the dimensions of 1D nanomaterials, are typically larger than 100 nm, and these typically have a rod-like structure, including CNTs, nanorods, nanowires, etc. [4]. 0D nanomaterials are spherical or nearly spherical and have dimensions that are no longer than 100 nm [5]. In simple terms, 0D nanomaterials are nanoparticles like carbon nanoparticles, fullerenes, inorganic quantum dots, carbon dots, quantum dots of graphene, etc. [6, 7].

Graphene, a one-atom-thick layered 2D sheet comprised of sp2-bonded carbon atoms is observed to be arranged as a honeycomb crystal lattice and features a variety of chemically reactive oxygen-containing groups, viz. epoxy, ether (-COC-), and hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the basal plane, and carboxylic acid (-COOH) groups on the periphery. Graphene has been an exciting material of significant interest and investigation for researchers worldwide owing to its exceptional thermal, optical, electrical, and mechanical features [8,9]. Graphene's insignificant bandgap, low absorptivity, restacking, and metal conductivity, however, significantly restricted it applications in practical materials and electronics [10,11]. Fortuitously, unlike graphene, Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have a non-zero bandgap [11] and are zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials made of carbon that contain a graphene lattice. They are highly soluble alternatives for graphene and exhibit superior solubility than carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which is an essential characteristic for a simple transition to commercial development [12,13]. According to Roy et al., 2021, GQDs offer great chemical inertness, superior biocompatibility, a wide surface area, high π-π conjugate bonding, better surface modification, non-toxic qualities, and can be used to generate energy in remote places without access to commercial facilities [14]. Lui et al., 2013, reported that by adding hetero atoms to the π-conjugated system, one can successfully modify the bandgap and fluorescence of GQDs [15] hence modifying the optical and electrical properties of the studied material. Owing to the GQDs' enormous surface area and better conductivity than graphene oxide (GO) [16], they are used as electrodes in capacitors [17], batteries [18,19], and other electronic devices.

Numerous studies (original work and reviews) have been carried out in the last few years targeting the preparation and specific applications of GQDs in diverse areas of applications. Fig. 1 represents the importance of GQDs as a potential material and hence has been widely studied in the last decade. However, most of the review studies are not focused on specific applications but rather represent the multiple applications of GQDs. Still, few of the potential reviews have focused on certain applications of GQDs viz. drug delivery, health care, environment, agriculture, energy storage, etc. as represented in Table 1 , which are a few of the most cited articles also.

The assessments on energy storage, however, have not studied all areas of energy storage and majorly limit their studies to LIBs and solar cells. An energy-focused review on GQD had been reported earlier by Bak et al., 2016 [37], which is limited to the study of capacitors, LIBs, and solar cells. These and many other review articles have explained the potential of GQDs in different energy sectors, nevertheless, the requirement for an updated, focused, and comprehensive assessment targeting various energy storage applications of GQDs is need of hour so that the research community finds the essence of opportunities provided and challenges faced by GQD based material in diverse forms of energy storage.

This study principally focuses on the synthesis and characterization of GQDs, with an emphasis on understanding their structural, optical, electrical, and electrochemical properties. These properties of GQDs are found to be crucial in establishing the relationship between the energy storage competencies of GQDs (and doped GQDs) and their structural characteristics. The review work presents a comprehensive analysis of the unique features of GQDs and mechanisms involved in driving the enhanced performance of GQDs for energy conversion and storage applications, and their potential applications in LIBs, SIBs, solar cells, supercapacitors, fuel cells, etc. Additionally, the study highlights novel findings, such as the surface functionalization of GQDs by heteroatom doping, and hybrid material strategies in optimizing GQD performance. This work generates a balanced and comparative perspective, thus benchmarking GQDs against other potential materials in terms of efficiency, scalability, and environmental impact.

The present study also provides a foresighted conversation on the potential of GQDs (doped and undoped) as an effectual material for energy storage and conversion technologies in various forms. This review critically examines the existing and upcoming challenges for GQDs, such as the need for economical and ascendable synthesis approaches, achieving batch-to-batch uniformity, and thus integrating various forms of GQDs into commercially viable devices for meeting industrial and societal demands. This review work is an attempt to identify challenges and propose scientific potential solutions, that not only advance the research on GQD-based energy materials but also offer a roadmap for imminent and innovative sustainable energy technologies. This holistic approach makes the work a significant contribution to the field, bridging the breach between fundamental science and applied applications.

For the effective applications of GQDs in different potential fields, the synthetic strategy applied plays a vital role. The choice of synthesis pathway depends upon the type and required application of GQDs. Among different strategies, the most frequently applied techniques are top-down and bottom-up approaches which are represented in Fig. 1B. In the top-down method, bigger graphene molecules are quickly and spontaneously fragmented into smaller ones, whereas in the bottom-up method, smaller molecules are combined to create a structurally stable substance.

The most common techniques for developing stable GQDs in the top-down approach include hydro/solvothermal, electrochemical, chemical oxidation, ultrasonic, and microwave synthesis. Due to their straightforward operations, these approaches have the advantage of being simple to scale up. Additionally, the existence of free functional groups makes them susceptible to additional modification using sophisticated techniques.

Due to its convenient and efficient techniques of synthesis, this method has been among the easiest methods to manufacture GQDs [38,39]. This process depends upon the concept that breaking the bonds between carbon materials at high pressures and temperatures will result in GQDs [40–42]. For solvothermal methods, dimethylformamide (DMF, an organic solution), is employed as the solvent instead of water, which is typically the case for hydrothermal methods. The most common starting material to generate GQDs is carbonaceous material graphene oxide (GO) [43]. The GO (precursor) molecules/sheets are initially exposed to strong oxidants, which cause them to produce numerous functional groups in the carbon matrix [44–47]. These functional groups aid in the cleavage of the precursor molecules into smaller ones, thereby producing GQDs. Moreover, heteroatom (nitrogen, sulfur, boron, fluorine, etc.) doped GQDs with unique physical and chemical features in terms of conductivity and charge distribution at carbon atoms can also be created using the hydro/solvothermal method [48–52]. The mechanism of cleavage of GO (a precursor molecule) is represented in Fig. 2 A as mentioned by Pan et al., 2010 [53].

The solvothermal approach facilitates the production of small and uniform GQDs by allowing the reaction to occur at relatively high pressure and temperature [56–59]. Zhou et al., 2014 employed a hydrothermal method, avoiding the use of concentrated acid for producing pure GQD thus preventing the inclusion of contaminants throughout the synthesis [60]. This strategy is associated with numerous advantages, such as the use of a simple experimental setup resulting in high quantum yield (QY) with a wide range of GQD particle sizes, low-cost production, and no obligation for purification or dialysis [61]. However, the combination of high temperature and pressure makes it dangerous, and it frequently takes time(at least 5 h)to produce GQDs [62].

This method for producing GQDs is based on the dissociation of the C-C bond utilizing strong oxidizing chemicals like hydrogen peroxide and sodium nitrate as well as strong acids like nitric acid and sulfuric acid [63]. The precursors utilized for the synthesis of GQDs are carbon fiber, carbon black (CB), and graphite powder [64–67]. The starting molecules are exfoliated, undergo oxidation and oxidative cleavage, and eventually undergo cleavage at the site of oxygen-based defect [68,69]. With this approach, it is possible to precisely tailor the GQDs' topographic height, average size/dimensions, inter-layer spacing, type of functional groups, and bandgap by adjusting factors like oxidative duration, oxidant types, or reductant concentrations. Numerous oxygen functional groups are present in the resultant GQDs, which is favorable for upcoming modification [69].

Aside from the advantages already discussed, this method has several drawbacks, such as acidic oxidation and exfoliation, which need working in hard conditions, high acid consumption, issues with acid removal, and a protracted reaction period as reported by Gong et al., 2017 [68]. Nevertheless, this method remains an efficient and relatively simple strategy for producing GQDs on a large scale using diverse carbon-based materials.

The chemical oxidation/exfoliation and the hydro/solvothermal method are efficient methods for creating GQDs, however, they take a prolonged time and have a low yield [70]. Due to the fast and uniform heating of the reaction media, the MW approach can significantly shorten the time for synthesis and significantly improve the yield in comparison with the hydro/solvothermal method, whereas an ultrasonic technique can produce a lot of tiny bubbles and release a lot of energy, making it easier to break bonds (C-C) and more advantageous to shear the carbon materials [70–72]. As shown in Fig. 2B, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be synthesized from graphene oxide (GO) using the microwave method. During this process, acid oxidation causes C-C bond breakage, resulting in a carbon skeleton with fewer epoxy groups and more carbonyl groups. This alteration weakens the graphitic domains, facilitated by microwave energy [54].

The MW and ultrasonic methodology also have the substantial advantage of making it possible to carry out some heteroatom doping, which is challenging to do with other synthesis techniques. With GO serving as the carbon source and borax serving as the boron source, GQDs doped with boron-doped (B-GQDs) are synthesized employing an MW and acid-free one-pot process. Here, defects including surface flaws, structural flaws, and linear flaws make the GQDs fragile and attackable, opening the door for B doping [73].

It should be noted that strong acids viz., H2SO4, HCl, HNO3, etc. are frequently used in MW and ultrasonic methods, which makes it challenging to eliminate the excess quantity of these acids from the prepared GQDs solution [54]. As a result, there is a great need to investigate other synthetic pathways to produce GQDs, as doing so, not only simplifies the process of GQD purification but also significantly minimizes the possibilities of environmental pollution. Taking these factors into account, graphitic crystalline GQDs with homogeneous dimensions (4–10 nm), are synthesized using an integrated O3/H2O2/ultrasound technique [74]. The •OH created by O3/H2O2 with the aid of ultrasound can oxidize and break down the bonds (C-C) close to the existing defects and sp3 carbons, thus representing a potential mechanism for creation.

Over the past few years, the electrochemical oxidation method has been established as a reasonably simple, promising, and productive method for the synthesis of GQDs. With the electrochemical oxidation technique as represented in Fig. 2C, several kinds of carbonaceous materials like graphite (powder/rod), graphene (flake/film/foam), and CNTs are exfoliated and sliced using a high voltage/current in an electrolyte [55,75–78].

The electrolytes used, applied voltage/current density, and the starting materials have a significant impact on the created GQDs' quality as well as the effectiveness of the electrochemical exfoliation process. Currently, two potential synthetic processes for electrochemical exfoliation have been put forth: (a) direct oxidation of C-C bonds by electrochemistry; and (b) oxidative cleavage by hydroxyl radicals (•OH) or oxygen radicals (•O) produced by oxidizing water [77,78].

In contrast to other synthesis techniques, this procedure does not call for sophisticated machinery and can be carried out in ambient settings with specific control over the size/dimension of the synthesized GQDs. Additionally, the electrochemical oxidation and cutting process does not require perilous chemicals or prolonged purification or separation processes [55]. Furthermore, GQDs with a relatively low defect density can be produced by electrochemical oxidation and cleavage in a time range of a few minutes to several hours. The main disadvantage of this method is, the production of low photoluminescent GQDs, the need for extra treatment to achieve complete exfoliation, and the difficulty of removing the high boiling solvents employed in the process to create "clean" GQDs. Moreover, the organic compounds or ionic liquids generated on the surface of GQDs by electrochemical reaction would somewhat change their properties [79–81].

Most top-down approaches produce a limited amount of GQDs because a very small portion of carbon-based source materials are transformed into GQDs during the process of synthesis. Furthermore, it frequently results in rich imperfections and destroys the structure of the produced GQDs to variable degrees. As a result, there is a significant motivation to look for additional synthetic alternatives that can be used to reliably produce superior GQDs on a wide scale, thus significantly evolving their practical applicability.

In this prospect, another appealing method for creating GQDs is the bottom-up strategy. Studies show that the synthesized GQDs with this approach have appropriate qualities, a decent size range, and a strong luminescence emission [82,83]. Small organic compounds and aromatic molecules can be classed as the synthetic precursors of GQDs. Currently, numerous synthetic methods have been established as effective means to create GQDs, including the microwave-aided hydro/solvothermal method, microwave pyrolysis, soft template approach, and molecular carbonization method.

Since hydrothermal methods frequently have slow reaction times, microwave technology in modern times has evolved as a quick heating technique that is frequently utilized for nanomaterials production. It boosted the yield while simultaneously reducing the reaction time [84].

It is possible to create GQDs by utilizing the designated MAH (Microwave-assisted hydrothermal) method, which combines the advantages of both, hydrothermal and microwave synthesis [85]. Quantum dots with a uniform size distribution are created by microwave heating, which offers simultaneous, uniform, and quick heating [86,87]. Additionally, sugars, are receiving great attention for the large-scale preparation of GQDs [88–92], GQDs have been synthesized using a simple MAH process, which majorly uses three distinct sugars as precursors (including glucose, fructose, and sucrose) [88,89]. MAH method was employed by Tang et al., 2013 to make water-soluble GQDs with glucose as a precursor as represented in Fig. 3 A, the GQDs loaded with different functional groups were thus prepared without the use of surface passivation agents or inorganic additions [90]. The intramolecular dehydration occurring in these sugars, that creates the GQD's nucleus, is thought to be the likely process for their synthesis, which leads to the gradual formation of GQDs. The hydrothermal technique produces GQDs with a high degree of crystallinity by putting the C-C bonds in a well-organized configuration under intense pressure. Zhao et al., 2017 used cheap deoiled asphalt (DOA) for the synthesis of high-quality GQDs using microwave-assisted one-step mild oxidation. This method helped in the formation of such GQDs which dissolved in water efficiently with lesser size than reported earlier [93].

Molecular carbonization is presented as an easy and safe approach for creating GQDs [95,96]. The fundamental concept behind employing this method is to use the appropriate organic polymers/compounds for further carbonization and dehydration [97]. The common precursors utilized to create GQDs via the molecular carbonization process are sugar, citric acid, and maize flour [98,99]. A common synthesis involved dissolving glucose granules in deionized water where the mixture was processed for 8 h at 200 °C in an autoclave by Bayat and Saievar-Iranizad 2017. On the formation of single-layered GQDs, the liquid sample initially presented as transparent (colourless) turned orange. The method is explained as follows: During the hydrothermal process, glucose molecules generate C=C by dehydration thus generating the fundamental building block of graphene and resulting in the production of the QDs. Finally, GQDs are created as a result of covalent interactions between carbon atoms [100].

The key advantages of the synthesized GQDs comprise high yield, low cost, and wide-scale production. They were uniformly dispersed without any evident aggregation. Furthermore, without the use of catalysts or molecular precursors, certain organic solvents having double bonds, double hydrophilic groups, or benzene rings directly can be converted into GQDs [99,101]. However, the use of the molecular carbonization process is quite difficult as it is not easy to accurately control the dimensions and structure of prepared GQDs thus generating GQDs of multi-dispersive nature [102–104].

Compared to traditional synthetic procedures, the soft template method is often a quick and efficient way to produce nanomaterials. The main advantage of this technology is its ability to effectively regulate the morphology, particle size, and structure of the nanomaterials. According to their varied structural characteristics, the template approaches are divided into a soft and a hard template form [104,105]. The manufacturing of GQDs is much better suited for the soft template method than the hard template method. According to Fig. 3B, Liu et al., 2011 created monodispersed GQDs of 60 nm having disk-like shape using hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) as a template and precursor [94].

In theoretical terms, graphene can be enclosed in fullerene quantum dots, which have a quasi-zero dimensionality. Notably, using this idea as a foundation, cage-opening fullerenes on ruthenium surface (Ru, serves as a catalyst) were used to create geometrically well-defined GQDs by Lu et al., 2011 [106]. A surface vacancy was created in the single crystal of Ru because of the strong C60-Ru interaction during the process, which also caused C60 molecules to become embedded on the surface. Bulk GQDs are produced by this approach which show symmetrical 3 and 6-fold structures, a relatively low edge free energy, and high thermal stability up to 727 °C and a narrow bandgap of 0.25–0.8 eV, while this synthesis also contains traces of parallelogram and trapezoid-shaped GQDs.

In conclusion, the top-down and bottom-up synthesis strategies are typically used to create GQDs. The easy availability of raw materials, cheap cost, and straightforward technological procedures with a high QY, that can be employed for wide-scale manufacturing, are the key benefits of the top-down approach. Large molecular compounds like graphite make up the majority of the frequently utilized raw materials. It is to be noted, that top-down approaches are unable to regulate the size of GQDs and many a time led to structural defects and generate heterogeneity in various properties. The hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis techniques are two of the top-down strategies that are employed most frequently.

Bottom-up procedures, as opposed to top-down methods, exhibit some remarkable benefits in manipulating the chemical composition, configuration, and physicochemical characteristics of GQDs through the careful choice of diverse organic precursors molecules and meticulously controlled synthetic processes and conditions. Particularly, by conveniently inheriting from the organic precursors, it is simple to create numerous heteroatoms doping GQDs. It offers tunable electronic, mechanical and optical properties by controlling size/dimension, shape, and functionalization. However, the bottom-up approaches typically include somewhat complex synthetic processes, GQDs with polydispersity, and a low QY. A Few of the top-down and bottom-up methodologies for GQD preparation are arranged in Table 2 .

There are a variety of methods for GQD preparation with a wide range of precursors, which offer GQDs with different components and complex structures. Despite their differences in structure and components, GQDs share certain common features which include tiny imperfections (defects), a mix of hybridizations (sp2 and sp³), and various oxygen-containing groups (-COOH, -OH, and C-O-C) [103]. It is also important to highlight that the physicochemical characteristics of GQDs are linked to their surface morphology and inherent structure, giving them significant potential for use in many industries, particularly in energy storage and energy conversion [104,121].

GQDs are crystalline structures generally comprised of 10 graphene layers, with a thickness of 20 nm [122]. In general, there is a strong correlation between synthetic approaches and the structure (size, composition, height, shape, and functional groups) of GQDs [123–126]. While bigger GQDs are often polygonal (rectangular, hexagonal, or triangular), smaller GQDs typically have elliptical or circular shapes. The Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) studies established that as the GQDs' size increases, their average height also increases [127,128]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) can be used to determine the crystallinity and microstructure of the GQDs. The degree of oxidation has a significant impact on their interlayer spacing in particular [129–132]. Additionally, characterization using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) can be advantageous to look further into their intricate crystalline structures [133]. The number of layers and degree of distortion in the carbon structure increases with increasing lattice spacing. Additionally, Raman spectroscopy is a valuable method for analyzing GQDs, particularly for establishing the degree of structural order between the crystalline and amorphous graphitic systems using the intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the D-band to the G-band [134].

Owing to, extended surface area, extremely small size, and tunable properties, GQDs can also be employed as anode and separator materials in LIBs. These unique properties of GQDs offer a short and quick transmission route and fast rate of conduction which make them a potential contender for energy storage applications.

Utilizing photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and absorption spectra in the visible and UV ranges, a thorough investigation of the optical characteristics of GQDs has been reported in many works. As a novel semiconductor material, GQDs display characteristic UV absorption, a long tail, and two small, broad peaks that can be seen in the GQDs absorption spectra [135]. The absorption spectra of GQDs were observed in the UV–Vis range of 230–320 nm, which may be attributed to π → π∗ (230 nm) transition and n → π∗ excitation (320 nm), respectively as shown in Fig. 4 A [136–138].

One of the many, intriguing characteristics displayed by the GQDs is photoluminescence. Depending on the synthetic approach, GQDs can emit characteristic radiations in the deep UV [89], blue [141], green [142–144], white [144], yellow [139], or red [145], region with a QY ranging from 2 % to 22.4 % [140,145]. With the increment in the excitation wavelength, the PL intensity of GQDs becomes weaker, which results in the shifting of peaks to longer wavelengths (Fig. 4B), clearly demonstrating an excitation-dependent PL behavior [53]. Internal factors (defects, size, and thickness of GQDs and type of functional groups present) along with external factors (concentration and pH of solvent, excitation wavelength, etc.) majorly affect the GQDs PL spectrum [124,146]. As represented in Fig. 4C among various internal aspects, the size of GQDs considerably influences the PL spectrum primarily due to quantum confinement effects [147,148]. The bandgap of GQDs decreases as their size increases [149], resulting in size-dependent excitation and emission spectra [139]. In the case of external features, the pH of the GQDs solution is a significant factor that impacts the PL spectrum [137] displayed in Fig. 4D, which is related to the change of GQD between active and inactive zigzag sites in different media [150]. Furthermore, the different types of solvent influence the GQDs PL spectrum greatly [140,151] (Fig. 4E), however, the behavior is not always dependent on the solvent [152]. The PL spectrum is also impacted by the concentration of the GQDs solution (Fig. 4F) [137].

The bulk properties of GQDs have been demonstrated to have excitation-dependent behavior in their PL spectra, which is most likely brought on by the differences present in the emissive sites and/or diverse sizes of the GQDs [153]. This hypothesis encourages investigators to precisely control the size and/or emissive sites of GQDs to address the excitation-dependent behavior. The defect state emission theory and intrinsic state emission theory effectively provide explanations, for how the PL properties of GQDs are significantly impacted by internal and external factors such as size, functional groups, concentration, pH, solvent, and excitation wavelength.

The size-dependent GQDs have profound implications for their applications in optoelectronics and other areas of interest for the scientific community. Factors like quantum confinement and tunable bandgap of GQDs make them suitable materials employed in LEDs, lasers, etc. Large-sized GQDs emit longer-wavelength (red-shifted) light, while smaller GQDs exhibit larger bandgaps and emit shorter-wavelength (blue-shifted) light. Furthermore, the size-dependent characteristics such as photoluminescence, high stability, high quantum yield, and ability to absorb/emit light across a wide spectrum provide significant advantages to GQDs over traditional materials in optoelectronic applications and make them an ideal candidate in domains of sensing, imaging, detectors, solar cells and displays [127,132].

A few nano-scaled GO molecules with oxygenated functional groups generate GQDs. The disordered sp2 carbon network at the basal plane, which promotes electron transfer, is formed by functional groups that contain oxygen. The presence of various/numerous functional groups on the surface of GQDs has a substantial impact on their electrical conductivity. This relationship can be understood through several factors: (a) significant quantum confinement and edge states arising due to structural limitations, (b) alterations in the hybridization of carbon atoms, (c) the creation of a barrier at the sites of functionalization and within the electron-potential range, and (d) the introduction of new energy stages designed due to functionalization [148,154].

Various synthetic processes generate edge effects in GQDs thus generating exceptional physical, chemical, and electronic properties at the boundaries (edges) of a material, which are distinct from its interior (bulk) properties. These edge effects have a critical role in establishing the chemical reactivity and hence the stability of GQDs [155]. Edges/boundaries in GQDs because of certain defects, sagging bonds, functional groups (e.g., -OH, -COOH), localized electronic states, and various forms of edges (armchair or zigzag) make them highly reactive and influence interactions. Owing to a higher edge-to-surface ratio, the stability of GQDs is observed to decline with decreasing size, however, the factors viz. functionalization and passivation can reduce the oxidative damage and improve the stability. In addition to chemical stability edge characteristics can affect the electronic and optical properties (bandgap and fluorescence) of GQDs thus making them critical energy storage applications [156].

The optical and electronic properties of GQDs are significantly impacted by quantum confinement as compared to materials like bulk graphene. The electronic structure is characterized by a zero bandgap in bulk graphene, with a continuous density of states, thus establishing it as a semimetal. In GQDs, spatial confinement of electrons is introduced due to the nanoscale size of the particles, which disrupts the delocalized π-electron system. The confinement of electrons leads to the formation of a tunable bandgap, which is inversely proportional to the GQD size. Thus, GQDs of different sizes exhibit semiconducting behavior, with exclusive electronic and optical features establishing them as highly suitable for applications in optoelectronics, batteries, and supercapacitors [156].

Likewise, the optical properties of GQDs, their electrical properties are also tunable probably due to the possibility of modification and doping of GQDs with different potential materials. The great probability of electron transport, fast electron exchange, and tunable bandgap offer great electrical and electrochemical conductivity to GQDs as compared to their other carbonaceous counterparts. These electrochemical properties and electrical properties of GQD-based electrode materials pave the route of their use in supercapacitors, batteries, fuel cells, etc.

In summary, aside from properties like edge effects and quantum confinement, the GQDs displayed several significant properties, including stable PL, inertness, excellent solubility, surface grafting, non-toxicity, biological compatibility, and electrical conductivity [157]. Some of these qualities are noticeably superior to those of conventional semiconductor quantum dots, leading to a variety of promising implementations of GQDs in bioimaging [126], solar cells [158–160], supercapacitors [161], photodetectors [162,163], fluorescent agents [164,165], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [166], batteries [167], sensors [168], drug carriers [169–171] and photo-/electro-catalytic [172] and so forth, as shown in Fig. 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of GQDs in different energy storage fields
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs' comprehensive characteristics (and those of functionalized GQDs) would be useful in several fields. GQDs offer an extensive range of potential uses in medicine [126,169–171], optics [162,163,166], and energy [158–161], all of which will have a substantial positive impact on our standards of life and draw significant financial interest. Understanding the distinctive characteristics of GQDs and modifying them using various methods, including doping, controlling shape or size, creating composite materials, etc., have been the focus of extensive research. GQDs might gain popularity as a material for cutting-edge technologies throughout the ensuing ten years. The applications of GQDs that are being used in fields connected to energy will be presented in this section as shown in Fig. 5.

To meet the energy demands of the current and future, it is critical to cultivate supercapacitor battery technology that has a lightweight design and demonstrates high efficiency, and high energy density, along with extended cycling life, and lasting stability. The energy harvested from natural resources (renewable/sustainable energy sources), requires a battery storage technique. Lately, the Na-ion batteries (SIBs) and Li-ion batteries (LIBs) have been the subject of extensive investigation because of their high energy density, extended cyclability, and affordability. Studies indicate that GQDs can be utilized to improve the existing battery's electrical capabilities.

Batteries are one type of energy storage device that efficiently transforms chemical energy into electricity through redox reactions or intercalation processes, as is typically the case with LIBs. Two electrodes are used in them, immersed in the electrolyte and separated via a suitable separator [173,174] as shown in Fig. 6 A. Our portable electronics (phones, tablets, laptops, etc.) and new-generation electric cars already run on LIBs, making them a part of our daily lives. They exhibit several benefits, including high working voltage and excellent cycling stability [175].

The energy output of LIBs is currently constrained by their slow Li-ion diffusion kinetics and relatively low theoretical capacity (372 mA h g−1), which limit their use in high-power systems [178,179]. In light of these considerations, it is highly desired to investigate innovative anode nanomaterials and nanocomposites to improve LIB performance. The unique physical and chemical characteristics of GQDs (a brand-new class of 0D carbon nanostructure), provide them a tremendous potential for use in energy conversion and storage systems. The restricted bandgap in the composite material is a result of quantum confinement encountered in GQDs, which alters the conductivity of electrons internally. According to reports present by Liu et al., 2013, Li-ion diffusivity may be influenced by quantum confinement, which may therefore have an impact on the electrochemical cycling of a battery over time and its overall performance. The electrochemical performance of LIBs is significantly improved by incorporating GQDs that accelerate the electrolyte transport and assist in large electron transfer [17]. As an electrode coating material, GQDs have ability to create a large surface area thus facilitating efficient ion transport between the active material and the electrolytic solution. This results in highly efficient energy storage and rapid energy release [180].

Zhang et al., 2019 reported a two-step electro-deposition process of C-GQDs (coal-based) over α-Fe2O3 using water and DMF as the electrolyte. According to the electrochemical test analysis, the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites achieve a specific capacitance of 1582.5 mA h g−1 at 1 Ag-1. Along with increased cyclability, the charge-discharge (CD) specific capacitance remained as high as 1320 mA h g−1 after 110 cycles. Additionally, at a high current density (5 Ag-1), the CD-specific capacitance of coal-based C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites reached 1091 mA h g−1, displaying optimal rate performance [158]. The storage capabilities of LIBs with B-GQD or N-GQD atoms were assessed by Saroja et al., 2020, where the undoped and doped GQDs (B-GQDs and N-GQDs) were utilized as pure anodes for LIBs [176]. The specific capacities of B-GQDs (1896 mA h g−1) and N-GQDs (1500 mA h g−1) are larger than those of undoped GQDs (697 mA h g−1). Furthermore, it was observed that B-GQDs and N-GQDs were able to retain high specific capacitances of 95.7 % and 90 % respectively, as compared to undoped GQDs (86 %) [176]. While the B-GQDs were observed to have significant rate capability and cyclic retention (Fig. 6B–C). Boron and nitrogen doping assist in boosting the electrochemical performance of GQDs for Na-ion and Li-ion storage. Doping with boron introduces p-type characteristics, thus improving ion interaction, while in N-GQDs nitrogen doping creates active sites for redox reactions and increases conductivity. The electrical conductivity, ion transport, and structural stability are greatly enhanced by the synergetic effect of doping via boron and nitrogen.

Heteroatoms are believed to enhance the Li-ion storage capacity of materials. This can be explained by doping the graphene framework with elements like boron and nitrogen, which create electron-rich and electron-deficient regions within the carbon lattice, thereby improving lithium-ion adsorption and storage capabilities. Table 3 highlights the performance results of GQDs in LIBs. In summary, incorporating GQDs consistently benefits lithium storage by increasing the specific surface area and introducing additional catalytically active sites. This not only enhances the electrode's specific capacity but also improves cycling stability by increasing the contact area between the electrolyte and the active material and boosting electrochemical conductivity [181,182]. These modifications induced in the structure of GQDs lead to higher storage capacities, better cycling stability, and improved rate performance. Overall, doping tailors GQDs into efficient materials for advanced energy storage applications.

Electronic devices working on LIBs are widely utilized nowadays, which has sharply increased demand. While reports point to a Li supply deficit, according to some estimates, by the year 2025 the demand will reach 900,000 tonnes per year, which would be 03 times higher than in 2018, as Li is not a naturally abundant element (0.002 % of the Earth's crust), its price will surge [191]. Owing to Sodium's (Na) higher natural availability and lower price, SIBs are becoming a more popular LIBs substitute. The operating principle of SIBs is the same as that of LIBs and is based on the intercalation of Na-ions, where the cathode materials and anode materials are used during the discharging and charging process respectively [177,192,193].

A range of materials for SIBs have been investigated, which could work potentially as effective materials for the cathode [194]. Chao et al., 2015 employed VO2@GQDs-supported graphene foam as the cathode material in that case. The electrode material rendered a high discharge capacity (306 mA h g−1) at a current density of 100 mA g−1 [18]. Finding suitable anode materials is the most difficult task in the development of SIBs, and GQDs are now being used as a dopant material in anodes as a result of recent advances.

For instance, a binder-free anode was developed by Kong et al., 2019 using arrays of Na2Ti3O7 nanofibers decorated with N-GQDs grown directly on flexible carbon textiles (Na2Ti3O7@N-GQDs/CTs). At 1 C current density a high initial discharge capacity (488 mAhg-1) was encountered for the developed anode material, while anode without GQDs exhibits a discharge capacity of 300 mAhg−1 (Fig. 6D). Na2Ti3O7@N-GQDs/CT retained a high discharge capacity (92.5 %) of its original capacity even after 1000 cycles, which is significantly greater than that of Na2Ti3O7/CTs which was only 68 % after 1000 cycles [177]. Thus the results infer that the addition of GQDs improves the electrochemical performance of SIBs, also the GQDs have a significant positive impact on the devices' cycling stability.

A flexible complete battery was also developed by Kong et al., 2019 where the Na2Ti3O7@N-GQDs were used as an anode while Na3V2(PO4)3@NC/CT was developed as a cathode. SIB thus developed as a full cell represents a high discharge capacity (104.8 mAhg-1) with an outstanding cycling performance (95.7 % after 50 cycles). Additionally, the fully charged battery has a greater power density of 5097.6 W kg−1 and an n density of 273.5 Wh kg−1 [177]. Nitrogen doping introduces additional active sites for Na-ion interaction, that permit rapid charge/electron transfer and ion diffusion, thus enhancing the electronic conductivity of GQDs. A superior rate capability, ultra-stable cyclabilities, and higher storage capacity are the consequences of N-GQD, thus showcasing the effectiveness of nitrogen doping in advancing energy storage materials.

To make a comparison between LIBs and SIBs, the electrochemical performance of the VO2@GQDs electrode supported by graphene foam was employed by Chao et al., 2015. At 100 A g−1, the electrode's specific capacity was 421 mA h g−1, while 94 % capacity retention after 1500 cycles at 18 A g−1 was also determined. The specific capacity for sodium storage was 306 mA h g−1 at 100 A g−1, and after 1500 cycles at 18 A g−1, 88 % of the initial capacity was still present [18]. To sum up, the SIBs storage technology appears to be a promising substitute for LIBs (Table 3), but it still requires further improvements to match the performance of LIBs.

For many years, the development of new technologies and devices has led to an increase in energy consumption. This has served as encouragement to find novel approaches for the generation of sustainable energy. Solar energy offers an abundant, affordable, safe, and clean energy source that can be converted into power without contaminating the environment or harming it. The photoelectric effect, which can be produced by photovoltaic materials and equipment, facilitates the transformation of sunlight into electrical energy [190]. Typically, the material out of which a device is made determines how well it performs. For the construction and characterization of devices, a variety of materials have been investigated. GQDs, a unique kind of 0D carbon nanomaterial, have gained tremendous interest in solar cell development in the last few years because of their alluring physicochemical and electrical characteristics. GQDs are highly versatile due to their unique optoelectronic properties, up-conversion luminescence, bandgap modifications, and cost-effective application. These characteristics make them easy to integrate into nanomaterials, enabling efficient charge/electron carrier extraction and enhancing their solar cell performance. GQDs hold significant promise for advanced photovoltaic systems, such as perovskite solar cells [195,196], organic-inorganic hybrid solar cells [197], and dye-sensitized solar cells [198,199]. In photovoltaic devices, GQDs are commonly used as sensitizers, electron/hole transport layers, or active layer additives because of their large surface area, high electron/charge mobility, and excellent ability to absorb and convert UV light into visible light [9].

Graphene sheets were utilized in solar devices before GQDs to significantly improve power conversion efficiency (PCE). However, the graphene sheet lacks an energy bandgap, which limits its direct applicability in nano-devices, this bandgap can be tuned which may be studied by turning 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs [146,200]. To address the growing electricity demand and enhance power conversion efficiency (PCE), extensive research has been conducted worldwide on various generations of solar cell devices. These include dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), polymer solar cells (PSCs), porphyrin-sensitized solar cells (PSSCs), quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs), inorganic solar cells (IOSCs), and organic solar cells (OSCs).

The efficiency of solar cells is generally determined by the absorption of incident photons and the amount of current generated. Key factors influencing solar cell performance include open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), and PCE (η). These parameters can be calculated using equations (2)–(4), respectively[201]

The exceptional performance, accompanied by simple preparation methods, PSCs have drawn considerable interest for decades [202]. Finding the right additives/fillers to complement PSCs to boost their efficiency is just as crucial as creating highly effective perovskite films [203]. Perovskite materials are categorized by a characteristic crystal structure represented as ABX3, where 'A', 'B', and ‘C’ are large cations, smaller metal cations, and an anion (usually halide) respectively. This structural formula of perovskite imparts high flexibility to them thus permitting a wide range of compositions with tunable properties causing alteration in bandgap (from 1.2 to 2.3 eV) by altering the composition of the components 'A', 'B', and 'X', making them ideal for applications in optoelectronics, especially in solar cells. The perovskite materials display characteristics like high light absorption efficiency, excellent charge carrier mobility, and scalable and economical fabrication have contributed to their quick adoption in solar cells and other optoelectronic devices [203].

Advanced nanomaterials with adjustable bandgaps and excellent chemical stability include GQDs and small graphene layers. In addition, GQDs perform excellently as electron donors and acceptors during photoexcitation [204].

Xie et al., 2017, investigated the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) utilizing SnO2/GQDs as the electron transport layer. The composite material demonstrated a significant improvement, achieving a maximum PCE of over 20.23 %, compared to the steady-state PCE of approximately 17.47 % observed with bare SnO2 films. Furthermore, it has also found that photovoltage decay was improved, going from 23.49 to 48.05 μs to 7.78 to 4.48 μs, and photocurrent decay was reduced to 7.78 to 4.48 μs, respectively [205]. Ryu et al., 2017 reported that TiO2 layers were modified with various-sized GQDs to produce effective planar perovskite solar cells. According to Fig. 7 A, GQDs improve the Voc and FF of the device by lowering the contact resistance and effectively transferring electrons across the interface of perovskite/TiO2. This may also raise the overall resistance of the said device by lowering the series resistance. The best PCE of 19.11 % was attained by the GQDs extending all the parameters of PV [206]. According to several studies because of the poor stability at high temperatures and humidity levels, organic-inorganic halide perovskites have not been developed to their full potential [207,208]. Bian et al., 2019 developed inorganic N-GQD-based g-CsPbI3 perovskite solar cells. When exposed to high temperatures and humidity, N-GQD material remains stable. Furthermore, the modified PSC demonstrated an increase in PCE from 15.53 % to 16.02 % and a considerable increase in Jsc (from 9.15 to 18.67 mA cm2, Fig. 7B) [209]. According to certain studies, the charge barrier present at the interface of TiO2/perovskite has been found to generate considerable electron buildup, which always persists and leads to increased hysteresis and decreased efficiency [210,211]. Additionally, TiO2 has a sluggish electron/charge mobility (about 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1) and needs to be sintered at an elevated temperature (around 400oC), which restricts the development of flexible PSCs [212,213]. In response to this, Zhou et al., 2019 introduced an easy method to fabricate composites of GQD and SnO2 nanoparticles that serve as electron transport layers (ETLs) for extremely effective, flexible, and rigid PSCs (Fig. 7C) [214].

Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are a type of solar cell developed as a cost-effective alternative to traditional photovoltaic devices, offering attractive power conversion efficiencies (PCE, η) [215]. The typical structure of a DSSC as illustrated in Fig. 8 A consists of a counter electrode (Pt), a dye-sensitized photoanode (TiO2 or doped TiO2), and a fluid electrolyte redox mediator (generally Co2⁺/Co³⁺, I₃⁻/I⁻, or Cu⁺/Cu2⁺ based) which fills the space between the anode and cathode, [216].

The intriguing characteristics of GQDs produce outstanding outcomes in many areas, particularly in energy conversion and storage. In contrast to the dyes, GQDs are able to generate a variety of electrons from a single photon. By producing more electrons to infuse into the TiO2 in this way, as opposed to the dye, the GQDs can improve the Jsc and PCE of the DSSCs. As a result, the GQDs function relatively similarly to the dye in DSSCs. In order to increase the activity of the photoelectrode and hence the effectiveness of DSSCs, it is therefore important to harness the exceptional qualities of GQDs, thus can be utilized in DSSCs. In this instance, GQDs and TiO2 are employed as co-sensitizer photoelectrode materials [216]. The TiO2/GQDs are employed as a photoelectrode and are the foundation upon which Fang et al., 2014 constructed the DSSCs. The outcomes showed that while the amount of dye utilized may be decreased, the characteristics of the photoelectrode could be greatly enriched by adding GQDs [218].

With the help of a hydrothermal technique, Liu et al., 2017 created SrRuO3(SRO) nanoparticles, which further were decorated with GQDs to create hybrid SRO-GQDs. By using this hybrid counter electrode material, the DSSC was able to obtain a PCE of 8.05 %, a significant increase over the reference counter electrode's (7.44 %) [199]. N719 and inexpensive single-layer GQDs (SLGQDs) based co-sensitized DSSC were developed and further doped with TiO2 that significantly enhanced Jsc and performance as compared to N719/TiO2, SLGQDs/TiO2 and TiO2. The results demonstrated that the high conductivity of SLGQDs and the cascaded energy level locations between the SLGQDs and N719 promote electron transfer, which could lead to a rapid electron/charge transfer into the conduction band of photoanode (TiO2). The best overall efficiency of 8.92 %, is produced by the co-sensitized DSSC which is 35 % higher than that of the N719/TiO2 DSSC [219].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic and inorganic hybrid solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: One of the optical uses of the GQDs is photon down-conversion, which enables them to absorb photons in regions of shorter wavelengths and emit photons in longer wavelengths. In silicon heterojunction solar cells, GQDs are employed as the down-conversion component. Adding GQDs in an amount of 0.3 wt% results in Jsc rising from 35.31 to 37.47 mA cm−2 [220]. By adding GQD to hybrid solar cells (Si/PEDOT:PSS) it attained an efficiency of 13.22 % [141]. The hybrid device (GQD/PEDOT:PSS) attains a high detection rate of 8 × 1011 Jones (unit of detection, cm·Hz1/2/W) without any bias voltage provided after GQDs are added as the photon down-conversion additive to PEDOT:PSS [221]. Table 4 lists the power conversion efficiencies and J-V characteristics of GQD-based solar cells.

Supercapacitors (SCs), better known as electrochemical capacitors or ultracapacitors, have achieved a lot of attention due to their exceptional qualities, including quick CD, high specific power, and longer cyclability. SCs are fabricated to have two electrodes (anode and cathode) that are separated from one another by an electrolyte and an ion-permeable separator, a systematic representation of the same has been shown in Fig. 8B [173].

Pseudocapacitors and electrical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) are the two primary categories of SCs. In pseudocapacitors, redox processes are used to store the charge, as opposed to EDLC, which stores the charge in a Helmholtz double layer (HDL) at the electrode-electrolyte interface [176,231]. Attributed to the lower energy density of SCs contrasts with batteries (Fig. 8C) they face a significant obstacle that prevents them from being used in certain applications [232]. To create and improve SCs' performances, significant research efforts have been made. This section of the review will focus on supercapacitors with more specialized designs made using GQD-based components.

GQDs are highly appealing conductive materials for supercapacitor applications ascribed to their exceptional electrical properties, numerous active sites, large surface area, excellent conductivity, and great solubility in various types of polar and organic solvents [233,234]. Table 5 summarizes the various GQD-based electrode materials potentially employed as supercapacitors. As a result, a lot of research has been carried out on new forms of capacitors, such as micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) and all-solid-state supercapacitors using GQDs materials. The specific energy of GQDs-based supercapacitors is comparable to that of batteries [235] which has been established by numerous studies, thus GQDs have been employed as an electrolyte and as a conductive electrode material for MSCs and SCs.

GQDs and N-doped rGO (NrGO) were coupled to create electrodes by Xu et al., 2017. The developed NrGO/GQD at a current density of 0.25 Ag-1 displays a high specific capacitance (344 Fg-1), and its capacitance has good 82 % cycling stability retained after 3000 cycles. However, the NrGO in the absence of GQD renders a lower specific capacitance (254 F g−1) at the same current density, and the developed electrodes exhibit noticeably better electrochemical performance [244]. In another work, after 5000 successive cycles, 98 % of the initial capacitance was conserved in a symmetric micro-supercapacitor developed by Liu et al., 2013 utilizing GQDs as electrodes, which had 534.7 μF cm2 at a current density of 15 μA cm2. The mentioned exceptional cycling stability and high-rate capability of the material are the results of the electrochemical test conducted on the built SC, which indicates that the judicious incorporation of GQDs improves the electrochemical activity of SCs [17].

An innovative approach to improve the electrochemical capabilities of activated carbon (AC) was carried out by Qing et al., 2019, by incorporating crystallized GQDs. At 1 A g−1, the constructed symmetric SC displays a high specific capacitance (388 F g−1) with outstanding cycle stability with no significant capacitance fading even after 104 successive cycles. The developed material renders an energy density of 13.47 Wh Kg−1 at a power density of 125 W kg−1 [245]. A unique supercapacitor was built using a CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite as an electrode material, that was obtained by using a hydrothermal method. It was observed that compared to CuMnO2, CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite has a considerably enhanced mass capacitance. An impressive capacitance of 520.2 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 was achieved by the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite in 3 M KOH electrolyte, along with this a remarkable rate capability and 78.4 % capacitance retention at a current density of 10 A g−1 was encountered. Furthermore, it allows the construction of asymmetric supercapacitors with a high capacitance of 153.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The CuMnO2/GQD electrode material at a high-power density (1108.1 W kg⁻1) delivered an energy density of 47.9 Wh kg⁻1. This SC material also exhibited cyclic stability (86.7 % after 5000 cycles) along with outstanding rate capability at a current density of 7 A g⁻1. The research represents the potential CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite by keeping LED on for >20 min [161].

Pseudocapacitors, contrastingly include asymmetric SCs and MSCs created with GQDs doped with sulfur or nitrogen. Multilayer hollow spheres of NiO@Co3O4 with GQDs nanoparticles (NiO@Co3O4@GQDs) were created by Yin et al., 2019, and their electrochemical performance was examined. NiO@Co3O4@GQDs has a remarkable capacitance of 1361 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 as a working electrode in a standard three-electrode cell with an appreciable retention rate (76.4 %) after 3000 continuous cycles at 10 A g−1. Compared to NiO@Co3O4, which only retains 54.7 % of its initial capacitance, this value is significantly greater. Additionally, utilizing an electrolytic solution of polyvinyl alcohol with KOH (PVA/KOH), they produced an all-solid-state hybrid SC made of NiO@Co3O4@GQDs and AC. The hybrid supercapacitor (ASC) device has a specific energy of 38.44 Wh Kg−1 at a specific power of 750 W kg−1, along with 123 F g−1 specific capacitance at 1 A g−1, and remarkable cycle stability (84 % retention after 104 cycles). This improved performance is attributed to the addition of carboxyl functionalized GQDs and bimetallic oxides (NiO and Co3O4), which enhance the active sites for electrochemical processes, that make ions easier to access, and improve electronic conductivity [188]. A SC was developed by Qiu et al., 2020 based on a flower-like GQD@Ni-Co layered double hydroxide (His-GQDs@Ni-CoLDH)@AC structure. The device's rendered a specific capacitance of 138 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, the developed SC provides a high specific energy (48.89 Wh Kg−1) at a specific power of 0.80 kW kg−1 and has decent cycling performance (93 % preserved after 6000 successive cycles). The His-GQDs/NiCoLDH's unique structure enables easy electrolyte ions transport attributed to its large surface area, which is likely the reason why the hybrid SC exhibits remarkable electrochemical characteristics. This is also a result of the addition of His-GQDs particles, which enhances the electrical conductivity of the electrode and prevents nickel and cobalt ions from expanding in volume during charge and discharge processes [13,246].

Fuel cells, with their high energy densities, affordability, and environmental friendliness, have emerged as the next big thing in energy storage and conversion, after solar cells and batteries [247–250]. A fuel cell is a device that uses the reaction between oxygen (O2) and fuel to convert chemical energy directly into power without the need for any intermediate energy sources [38,251]. The electrochemical reaction of O2 with fuel results in the production of electrical energy along with water, carbon dioxide, and some residual heat. The by-products generated with this reaction are much less as compared to conventional combustion processes [252]. Likewise, as in batteries and SCs, fuel also comprises two electrodes an anode and a cathode, the anode bipolar plates allow fuel to enter the fuel cell, while the cathode allows O2 to flow [253,254]. Fig. 8D depicts a schematic diagram of a typical fuel cell [217].

However, the uses of fuel cells are limited because of their low durability and delayed oxidation reaction rate (ORR) of the cathode, due to problems with O2 adsorption, O-O bond cleavage, and oxide removal [247–250]. Although precious metals are valuable ORR catalysts, they are not widely employed due to their high cost and restricted availability [248–250] Therefore, it is imperative to develop a potent and highly active ORR catalyst that effectively cleaves the O-O bond and expedite the chemical energy conversion to electrical energy.

Since GQD has a high electron transfer rate and an active surface area, becomes an ideal material for electrochemical devices. Moreover, 0D carbon products like GQDs offer numerous active electrochemical sites for the reaction owing to the presence of heteroatoms like nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen, phosphorus, boron, etc. [255]. In addition, heteroatom doping is a successful method for enhancing the ORR performance of carbon materials. This is achieved by changing the electron dispersal, electrical conductivity, and defects present in the carbon framework, which enhances the process of adsorption/desorption of O2 and O-O bond-breaking processes, ultimately increasing the electrocatalytic activity [256]. Hence, it is undeniable that GQDs can assist as a potential substitute, particularly to precious existing metal-based catalysts, for various energy storage devices including fuel cells. Liu et al., 2024 have demonstrated the use of GQDs of high entropy for their oxygen reduction reaction. The GQDs worked as metal-free catalysts in an alkaline electrolyte medium. The graphite-like structure has six catalyst elements with numerous dopants and functional groups on the surface. This gave the high entropy and highly active surface for electrochemical reactions which helped in the measurements of ORR. The successful doping of heteroatoms on GQD's structure helped in the selective catalytic activity with great durability [257]. fluorine and nitrogen co-doped GQDs (F/N/GQDs) were successfully synthesized by Sun et al., 2024 for effectual ORR catalysis. The electronic structure of the GQD was modified by the atoms of F and N, leading to synergistic improvement in catalytic performance for ORR. As compared to the GQDs and N-doped GQDs the onset potentials of F/N/GQDs were positively shifted by 200 mV and 170 mV in Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) respectively, while the positive shifting by 200 mV and 180 mV were encountered in half-wave potentials for F/N/GQDs (0.81 V). The results show that the modification significantly improves the ORR and establishes F/N/GQDs as comparable to commercially available Pt/C electrocatalysts [258].

For the fabrication of in-situ grown B-GQDs implanted FeCoMOF on NF (B-GQDs/MOF-t), a hydrothermal and electrodeposition process was applied by Yan et al., 2022. B-GQDs' surface modification increased the number of edge active sites and surface functional groups. Additionally, it produced a synergistic effect between FeCoMOF and B-GQDs that is advantageous in enhancing electrocatalytic activity towards ORR and surpassing commercially used Pt/C electrocatalysts. With the B-GQDs/MOF-15 cathode, the MFCs can attain an extreme specific power of 703.55 mW m−2, which is recorded to be 1.53 times higher than the commercially used Pt/C cathode. Furthermore, the cathode of B-GQDs/MOF-15 exhibits remarkable stability for 800 h, whereas for the cathode (Pt/C) the initial voltage diminishes to 61 % [259]. A novel composite (S, N-GQD/TiO2/C-800) composed of co-doped GQDs of nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) with TiO2 was developed. The possibility of using this composite as an ORR catalyst is investigated. With an appreciable initial potential (0.91 V), the synthetic composite exhibits exceptional electrocatalytic ability. Even after 2000 CV cycles, the half-wave potential of the developed catalyst shifts by just 65 mV, and after 15 h of stability testing, it retains 80 % of its relative current density. The catalyst has excellent stability in an alkaline media and has a high methanol tolerance. The well-developed interaction between the S, N-GQD/TiO2, and the C800 is responsible for the strong ORR activity of the developed composite. The unique structure of the TiO2 interface and S, N doped GQDs are advantageous due to their enhanced electrical conductivity with an increase in surface area. Also, it offers great charge transfer kinetics to the S, N-GQD/TiO2 from the C800 surface [260].

GO and GQD were added to a hyperbranched macromolecule (HBM) by Xu et al., 2021 to improve the membrane's selectivity for protons and methanol. By interacting through the hydrogen-bond interaction, the available functional groups on the surface of GQD and GO work with the sulphonyl (-SO3H) groups present in HBM to produce a proton-conductive channel. Extensive phase separation of the composite membrane is successfully avoided by using GQDs that are smaller in size than GO. For the GQD-HBM the proton conductivity was achieved as 0.30 S/cm which is 17 % higher than pristine HBM. The physical barrier of GQD causes a substantial reduction in methanol permeability by ca. 50 %. As a result, the direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) fitted with the HBM/GQDs membrane renders a maximum specific power of 20.5 mW cm−2, improving the proton/methanol selectivity by more than 80 % representing an exceptional fuel cell performance [261]. Using an impregnation and hydrothermal technique, Faraji et al., 2018 synthesized non-Pt Pd-Co bimetallic Fe-N-doped GQD to be used as a nano-catalyst. Concerning methanol tolerance and robustness, the 10 wt% Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD catalyst exhibits performance comparable to that of commercially used electrocatalysts (20 % Pt/C). Because the catalyst has several active sites, the two-fold action in catalytic activity may be responsible for the outstanding catalytic activity displayed by 10 wt% Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD electrocatalyst [262].

Sun et al., 2017 synthesized a hybrid of rGO/N-GQDs with a roughly 2–6 nm diameter using two simple hydrothermal processes. The rGO/N-GQDs demonstrate an appreciable resistance to the methanol crossover, great electrocatalytic activity, and long-range stability. Furthermore, the electrocatalytic performance can be enhanced by adjusting the pyridinic-N content in the N-GQDs samples. Also, the initial potential is nearly proportionate to the electron transfer number of the pyridinic-N in the N-GQDs. Such types of metal-free electrocatalysts could be helpful in the progressive development of fuel cells and metal-air batteries [263]. A simple and affordable catalyst based on polyoxometalate (NaPWO) functionalized GQDs has been developed by Colak et al., 2016. The catalysts include nanoparticles (NPs) of platinum (PtNPs), palladium (PdNPs), and platinum-palladium (Pt/PdNPs). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results indicated that the synthesized bi-metallic nanoparticle-based nanocomposites were catalytically active towards methanol as fuel. In particular, the PtPdNPs/NaPWO/GQDs catalyst offers a chance to build a capable electrode material with a larger surface area than PtNPs/NaPWO/GQDs, PdNPs/NaPWO/GQDs, NaPWO/GQDs, and GQDs catalysts, as well as superior tolerance for CO and excellent electro-oxidative performance [264]. GQDs/f-MWCNTs composite with an active surface area of 119 m2 g−1 was synthesized as an electrocatalyst for DMFC application by Genistein et al., 2020. An electro-oxidative activity of 0.5 M and a high tolerance for CO were observed for the electrocatalyst with If/Ib (current density ratio of forward and backward anodic peak) of 3.83 [265].

GQDs have extraordinary features that apply to numerous technological domains; therefore, they have been proposed as a new generation of carbon material. But for it to have a promising future, some shortcomings must be overcome. It is still being worked on and is expected that a thorough understanding of the electrical conducting qualities of GQDs will be achieved. Although numerous methods for synthesizing GQDs and doped GQDs (with N, S, B, etc.) have been documented thus far, despite several drawbacks with these current techniques, including their generally intricate synthetic processes with low yield, high cost, due to the use of expensive equipment's and processes, and extreme conditions, which somewhat impede the development as well as practical applications of GQDs. Additionally, the harsh reagents such as powerful oxidizers and strong acids employed in the synthesis procedure, may disrupt the carbon skeletons of GQDs and cause numerous flaws and unwanted functional groups, that can influence both their physical and chemical properties. In this regard, it is vital to investigate some straightforward, eco-friendly, practical, and affordable ways to create high-quality GQDs. The physical and chemical characteristics of GQDs have been investigated using simulations and experiments, but it is yet to be completely understood how the size, crystallinity, edges, defects, and heteroatom doping of GQDs used in various energy storage and conversion devices affect their electrochemical performances. The electrochemical energy storage and electrocatalysis fields have shown tremendous promise for GQDs-based nanocomposites. However, their potential uses in these energy/power-driven industries until now are in their early stages and are facing plenty of challenges. Strong supporting facts and compelling justification are still missing in these areas. In this situation, thorough investigations, and revelations of the functions of GQDs by theoretical and experimental designs are extremely desirable in electrochemical energy storage. The development of LIBs using GQDs as the electrode material is still in its infancy. Additionally, a paucity of organized studies on the assembly behavior, interaction, and reaction processes of GQDs with other electroactive materials is observed, each of these is essential for the potential design and manufacturing of enhanced GQDs-based electroactive materials for electrodes or electrocatalysts. GQDs for energy storage devices still face challenges like low synthesis yields, high cost, complex processes, and difficulty in achieving consistent size and functionalization. As discussed earlier the existing methods often demand harsh chemicals and accurate reaction controls, making scalability and cost-efficiency problematic thus diminishing the probability of large-scale production. Progressions in green, high-yield synthesis procedures are indispensable for industrial and societal applications. However, the conducting polymer and GQDs hybrid (CP/GQDs may result in electrode material that could satisfy various needs of imminent power sources, including high energy and power densities, increased electrode cyclability, electrical/electrochemical conductivity, safety, environmental friendliness, etc.

Therefore, there is a pressing need to thoroughly investigate how GQDs interact with other substances and in what way that interaction affects the characteristics of composite materials. The link between the electrochemical characteristics, the morphology, and the structure of GQD-based materials is very relevant and valuable since it reveals the electrochemical performances along with the energy storage mechanisms of these potential materials. To comprehensively examine the influence of so plenty of variables on their general electrochemical performances, the devices still need a significant amount of experimental investigation. Understanding the basic and functionalized GQDs that are evolving in the various stated applications and looking into how their performance is affected is important. This aspect of the difficulties offers greater opportunities for scientists engaged in material-based research, who firmly think that GQDs can meet the increased demands in future applications.

GQDs have established themselves as a potential rising star among the various carbon-based materials generally employed for societal and scientific endeavors, which can be produced through numerous scientific approaches, leading toward advanced optical, electrical, and electrochemical characteristics. GQDs display extreme potential to be used in several energy-related applications, particularly those involving batteries, solar cells, supercapacitors, and fuel cells. The findings discussed in this review represent the potential of GQDs (doped or undoped) to significantly enhance the longevity and electrochemical performance of electrode materials, thereby achieving optimal performance when applied judiciously. The extreme performance of GQD-based electrode material has been found to bestow high energy and power densities thus meeting the energy demands of our technology-driven society. While GQDs outperform traditional quantum dots and other carbon-based nanomaterials, there are still several unanswered questions that must be addressed before these innovative nanomaterials can be fully utilized. Easy purification techniques are needed to get rid of any remaining chemicals that could cleave the carbonaceous surface. Alternatively, creative ways to get a very high yield without removing any beginning ingredients are desirable. To get homogeneous attributes of GQDs, new methods for distinguishing between GQDs of various sizes are also necessary. The review highlights GQDs' transformative potential for sustainable energy technologies and identifies future directions for advancing their multifunctional applications. Overall, the authors believe that this significant overview and advancement can offer helpful recommendations and fresh perspectives to scholars working on the development and design of high-performance electrochemical energy storage.

Preeti Sharma: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Parul Yadav: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Anil Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis. Harish Mudila: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

Not applicable.

ce:section.ce:para: GQDs = Graphene Quantum dots, CNTs = Carbon nanotubes, GO = Graphene oxide, rGO = reduced GO, LIBs = Lithium-ion batteries, SIBs = Sodium-ion batteries, DMF = Dimethylformamide, CB = carbon black, MAH = Microwave-Assisted Hydrothermal, HBC = Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene, NMP = N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone, NIR = Near Infrared, AFM = Atomic force microscopy, XRD = X-ray diffraction, TEM = Transmission electron microscope, UV = Ultra violet, PL = Photoluminescence, QY = Quantum yield, LEDs = Light-emitting diodes, C-GQDs = coal based GQDs, N-GQDs = Nitrogen doped GQDs, B-GQDs = Boron doped GQDs, CTs = Carbon textiles, PCE = Power Conversion Efficiency, QDSCs = Quantum dot solar cells, DSSCs = dye-sensitized solar cells, OSCs = organic solar cells, IOSCs = inorganic solar cells, PSSCs = Porphyrin sensitized solar cells, PSCs = Perovskite Solar Cells, Voc = open-circuit voltage, Jsc = short-circuit current density, FF = fill factor, PV = Potential-voltage, ETLs = Electron transport layers, SLGQDs = single-layer graphene quantum dots, SRO = SrRuO3, N719 = (Di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato)ruthenium(II)), PEDOT:PSS = Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate), J-V = Current-voltage, SCs = Supercapacitors, EDLCs = Electrical double-layer capacitance, HDL = Helmholtz double layer, MSCs = micro-supercapacitors, NrGO = Nitrogen-doped rGO, PVA = polyvinyl alcohol, ASC = asymmetric supercapacitor, His-GQDs = Hitidine-doped GQDs, LDH = Lactate dehydrogenase, LD-GQDs = Low defect GQDs, HD-GQDs = High defect GQDs, RHAC = Rice Husk-based Activated carbon, CVD = Chemical vapor deposition, PCBM = phenyl C61 butyric acid methyl ester, NSGQDs = N, S co-doped GQDs, IL = Ionic liquids, WM@GQD = Tungsten disulfide-molybdenum copper oxide@GQD, PEG = Polyethylene glycol, TCPPZn = zinc tetrakis (4-carboxy phenyl)porphyrin, ZIF-8 = Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (a MOF), CNF = Carbon nanofibers, Fc = ferrocenyl.
